nativeRange.habitat: Found in subtidal coral reef zones, growing attached to rubble or rocky seafloor in areas with slow to moderate water movement, from the low-tide mark down into the upper subtidal zone.

imageCandidates.notes: StinaTano, 3648x2736. Vibrant lime-green spiny Eucheuma denticulatum clump held underwater in Zanzibar cultivation; sharp, whole thallus in frame, clearly shows the green coloration and spiny branching that define the species. Strongest single specimen shot.
StinaTano, ~1024x768. Alternate underwater view of a bright green E. denticulatum clump alongside a brown/tan colour form, showing both morphs and the fine spiny branches.
Derek Keats, 887x1384. Wild in-situ pale/tan branching Eucheuma thallus in open blue water (Philippines); shows the spiny, spreading branch habit against a reef background.
Coral Reef Research Foundation, 1296x863. In-situ wild tan spiny Eucheuma mat on the reef; close view emphasizing the dense fine spinelets covering each branch.
Clu028, 2048x1536. Fresh green spiny Eucheuma thalli on cultivation lines; close detail of the branching, spiny green fronds (some PVC/rope clutter in frame).

summary: Green Spiny Seaweed is a rare and challenging macroalgae from the Indo-Pacific. Despite its green coloration — a result of reduced red pigmentation — Eucheuma belongs to the red algae group, producing upright, spiny branches. It is rarely offered through specialty retailers. The genus is a major commercial source of carrageenan.
careNotes: Despite the trade name 'Green Spiny Seaweed', Eucheuma is a red alga (Rhodophyta, family Solieriaceae) — the green coloration results from a lack of the usual red pigment rather than belonging to the green algae lineage. In the wild, Eucheuma thrives in warm Indo-Pacific tropical reefs (within ~20° of the equator), anchored to rubble or rocky substrate in moderate flow. Standard reef water parameters are generally appropriate: temperature around 27–30°C, salinity around SG 1.023–1.026, and stable alkalinity. Moderate lighting is typically recommended for this genus. The genus Eucheuma (especially E. denticulatum and E. cottonii / Kappaphycus alvarezii) is commercially farmed at massive scale in the Philippines, Indonesia, and Tanzania as a carrageenan source used in food, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals. Aquarium propagation is typically by fragmentation, mirroring commercial farming practice. Herbivorous tankmates — including tangs, rabbitfish, and sea urchins — may consume or damage this alga. Eucheuma is rarely available in the aquarium hobby, with only a handful of specialty retailers carrying it at any given time. As a red alga, this species will MELT at the base if buried in sand — attach to rock via superglue, zip-tie/fishing line, or wedging instead. (Unlike green algae such as Caulerpa, Halimeda, Penicillus, Rhipocephalus, and Udotea, red and brown macroalgae are generally not suited to sand-bed placement.)
breedingNotes: In nature, Eucheuma completes a triphasic life cycle, cycling through three distinct stages (gametophyte, carposporophyte, and sporophyte) as part of sexual reproduction. This process has not been documented in home aquaria. In captivity, propagation is generally achieved by fragmentation — detaching healthy branch tips and securing them to rubble or mesh until they attach on their own.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source; provided species identity, coloration note, and difficulty rating.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Eucheuma
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Solieriaceae, Gigartinales, Rhodophyta), native range (Indo-Pacific tropical), habitat (subtidal reef, 0–10m), temperature range (27–30°C), salinity (30–35 ppt → SG 1.023–1.026), carrageenan commercial use, and fragmentation propagation. Used as cross-verification source for all high-stakes fields.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
